title: loadLibjxlModule has a TOCTOU window: concurrent callers can each create a new promise before either stores it
description: The guard `if (modulePromise === undefined)` and the subsequent `modulePromise = p` are not atomic. In a JavaScript environment a single microtask tick separates them, but `loadLibjxlModule` is called from async generators (events(), chunks()) that can interleave. The `.catch()` cleanup `if (modulePromise === p) modulePromise = undefined` correctly handles the happy-path retry, but there is a narrow window where two concurrent callers both see `undefined`, both create a fresh promise, the second overwrites the first, and the first promise's rejection no longer clears `modulePromise` (because `modulePromise !== p` for the first caller). The net effect is a spurious double-load on cold-start, not a persistent corruption, but if the first load resolves before the second and the second load fails, the rejection clears the cache and the successfully-resolved first promise is abandoned (memory/handle leak for the WASM module instance).

title: LibjxlEncoder.pushPixels chains onto pendingPushPromise but the outer await is on a different (non-chained) promise, so a concurrent second pushPixels races the first
description: In `pushPixels`, the method constructs `pushTask` as a `.then()` on the current `pendingPushPromise`, assigns `this.pendingPushPromise = pushTask.catch(...)`, then does `await pushTask`. A second concurrent call to `pushPixels` will read `this.pendingPushPromise` as the value it was before the first call updated it (the first assignment happens synchronously before the first `await`, which is correct), but `queuedPixelBytes += view.byteLength` is incremented on the synchronous path before the async push task runs. If two callers call `pushPixels` concurrently (e.g., from `Promise.all`), both increment `queuedPixelBytes` before either task runs. If the WASM module init fails after the second call increments the counter but before the first task validates it, the `queuedPixelBytes !== pixelByteTotal` guard in `chunks()` will see the double-counted value and throw incorrectly. More critically, the `streamingInputActive` branch inside the chained task reads `this.streamingInputActive` and `this.wasmEncState` which are set by `initModule()` asynchronously; if two pushes race through `ensureModule()` both waiting for the same `moduleInitPromise`, they both proceed into the WASM write concurrently with shared `wasmEncState`.

title: initModule leaks WASM encoder state when cancel() is called between module load and state creation
description: `initModule()` calls `await loadLibjxlModule()`, then checks `if (this.cancelled) return module` — but at this point the WASM encoder state (`this.wasmEncState`) has NOT yet been created. The early return is correct for that case. However, if `this.cancelled` becomes true AFTER `_jxl_wasm_enc_create_image*` allocates `this.wasmEncState` (i.e. the cancel races the synchronous-but-expensive state creation), `initModule` assigns `this.wasmEncState` and returns the module without calling `freeWasmState()`. The caller (`pushPixels`) then checks `if (this.cancelled) return` and exits without invoking `enc_free`. The state is leaked until `dispose()` or `cancel()` is called on the encoder, but `cancel()` itself calls `freeWasmState()` which does check `wasmEncState !== 0`, so the leak is bounded to cases where the caller abandons the encoder object without calling `cancel()` or `dispose()`.

title: LibjxlEncoder.finish() resolves finishResolve but does not guard against double-call, allowing chunks() to proceed prematurely if finish() is called twice
description: `finish()` sets `this.finished = true` and calls `this.finishResolve?.()`. There is no guard preventing a second call to `finish()` after `chunks()` has already started consuming output. If `finish()` is called twice in rapid succession (a usage error, but not checked), the second call resolves `finishResolve` again — but at that point `finishResolve` should already be null (cleared in `waitUntilFinished`), so the second call is a safe no-op. The more subtle issue: if `finish()` is called before `pushPixels` has completed all in-flight tasks (since `finish()` is synchronous), `chunks()` will `await pendingPushPromise` to drain them. This is correct. However, there is no mechanism to prevent calling `pushPixels` after `finish()` — the `this.finished` check at the top of `pushPixels` guards against it, but only after the byte-count increment has already happened (`this.queuedPixelBytes` is incremented before the `finished` check at line 1925). A push after finish increments the counter and then silently discards the data, leaving `queuedPixelBytes` higher than actual bytes in WASM, causing the `queuedPixelBytes !== pixelByteTotal` assertion to fail in `chunks()`.

category: shared mutable state / non-thread-safe module-level variable
title: setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting and setForcedTier mutate module-scoped state shared across all decoder/encoder instances in the same worker
description: `testModuleFactory`, `modulePromise`, `_forcedTier`, and `_cachedDetectedTier` are module-level variables mutated by `setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting()` and `setForcedTier()`. In a single-threaded JS worker this is not a data race, but it is a classic check-then-act problem: if test A calls `setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting(factoryA)` and then `await loadLibjxlModule()`, a concurrent test B (in the same Bun/Vitest worker process running tests in parallel) that calls `setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting(factoryB)` between the check and the store of `modulePromise` will poison the module cache for test A. The `afterEach` cleanup in the test suite calls `setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting(null)` which clears `modulePromise`, but since tests run in sequence within a single worker there is no actual parallel race. This is an architectural smell that would become a real race if tests are ever parallelised across workers sharing the module.

title: freeWasmState called from cancel() may double-free enc_free if chunks() generator is currently inside the streaming encode finally block
description: `cancel()` calls `freeWasmState()` which calls `module._jxl_wasm_enc_free!(this.wasmEncState)` and zeroes `this.wasmEncState`. Meanwhile, the `chunks()` generator's `finally` block (line 2111) also calls `this.freeWasmState()`. If `cancel()` fires while `chunks()` is suspended at a `yield` inside the streaming encode path (between `enc_take_chunk` calls), both code paths will reach `freeWasmState()`. The `wasmEncState !== 0` guard in `freeWasmState()` makes the second call a no-op (since the first zeroes the field), so the actual double-free is prevented. However, the `enc_take_chunk` loop in `chunks()` will call `module._jxl_wasm_enc_take_chunk!(this.wasmEncState)` AFTER the state has been freed by `cancel()`, because `this.wasmEncState` was already zeroed to 0 by `freeWasmState()`. The loop condition checks `chunkHandle !== 0` which `enc_take_chunk(0)` — calling with a freed/null state pointer — may behave undefined in C++. The `if (this.streamingInputActive && this.wasmEncState !== 0)` guard at line 2090 is checked before the loop, not during.

category: resource leak / WASM heap dangling view
title: takeBufferView returns a HEAPU8.subarray (dangling view) then frees the WASM handle in its finally, invalidating the view before callers can use it
description: `takeBufferView` (line 2603) returns a `LibjxlBuffer` whose `.data` is a `HEAPU8.subarray(...)` — a live zero-copy view into the WASM heap. The function frees the handle in its `finally` block. The caller therefore receives a `Uint8Array` that points into memory that has just been freed. While the WASM `_free()` call does not zero the memory, a subsequent WASM allocation can reclaim and overwrite the same region before the caller processes the bytes. This function is defined but the search shows no call sites in the current source (it appears unused or was replaced by `retainBufferView`). If it is called in any future code path, callers will silently read stale or corrupted data.

title: LibjxlDecoder.cancel() wakes the event loop but the WASM progressive decoder state is not freed on cancel
description: `cancel()` sets `this.cancelled = true` and calls `wake()`. The `eventsProgressive()` generator checks `!this.cancelled` at the top of its while loop and returns early. The `finally` block in `eventsProgressive()` correctly calls `decFree(dec)` to release the WASM decoder state. However, `cancel()` is also callable from `dispose()`, and if the events generator is currently suspended at `await this.waitForQueueItem()` (line 1496), the wake resolves the promise and the next loop iteration checks `cancelled` and exits cleanly via the `finally`. The risk is that if `dispose()` is called after `events()` has yielded its last event but before the generator has fully returned (i.e. the generator is suspended at the final `yield ev` for the `final` event on line 1711), the generator may not run its `finally` immediately. In JavaScript, `finally` runs when the generator is iterated to completion (`.next()` after the last yield or `.return()` is called on the iterator). If the consumer abandons the iterator without calling `.return()`, the `finally` and `decFree` never run. The WASM decoder state leaks until GC (which does not call finalizers for WASM memory).

title: writeIndexedDbModule is fire-and-forget with a swallowed catch, preventing tests from detecting IDB write failures
description: `loadBrowserModule()` calls `writeIndexedDbModule(key, module, options).catch(() => {})` (line 74). The intent is that IDB persistence is best-effort (quota/incognito). However, the empty `.catch` also swallows logic errors (programming mistakes in `writeIndexedDbModule`, `txComplete` rejection from IDB abort, etc.) that should surface during development. This is a minor operational concern in production but makes the loader harder to test correctly; it also means any concurrent write failure (e.g., two workers both loading the same WASM for the first time and both trying to write IDB) is silently ignored.

title: loader.ts accepts an AbortSignal but does not plumb it through IDB operations, so abort does not cancel an in-progress IndexedDB read
description: `LoaderOptions.signal` is passed to the `fetch()` call (line 68 in loadBrowserModule) and to `fs.readFile` in Node, but `readIndexedDbModule` and `writeIndexedDbModule` open the database and perform transactions without checking `signal.aborted` or subscribing to `signal.addEventListener('abort', ...)`. If the caller aborts during a slow IDB open (e.g., first cold start on a loaded device), the fetch is aborted but the IDB open and get proceed to completion before the module load resolves. This means cancellation latency is dominated by IDB, not the abort signal. Adding `requestToPromise` variants that reject on signal abort would make cold-start abort responsive.
